Alright, there are about four steps left and I am going to go over three of them right now because at this point, like I said, the hard part has been done. So the last thing we spoke about was assigning a protein and a caloric load, which is alright, point in grams of protein per pound, move out, fill the rest in with calories, swing calories. Now when you meet with them this time, at this point, the client is probably going to be pretty darn hungry because they've been dieting for the last 10 weeks or so, nine weeks. And what we want to do now is increase their engagement and give them another tool to help control their hunger. So at this point, I'm sure now they understand they're like, whoa, vegetables are way more filling than pancakes. And so like food calorie density is important. And now what we can do is we can incorporate meal timing. And in this context, it's really more like intermittent fasting. So now that they have a protein load and a calorie load, what you can do is you can shrink the eating window they have. And so they are consuming all of their meals or all of their calories within about six or 10 hours, depending on how hungry the person is. So if the person's like ready to gnaw their arms off, then this is where you want to shrink the feeding window to probably about six hours. And if the person's like, eh, I'm good, you can not go into intermittent fasting at all, or you can have a larger window of about 10 hours. And so this, but I actually have another video in the group about the benefits of intermittent fasting. So I guess I will just link that. I won't justify it any more than that. But basically intermittent fasting is a great tool to help stay on plan and stay full, highly recommended, especially if a person has been dieting for a minute. All right, moving right along after they do that for a couple more weeks. Now this is where things can get a little spicy. You can keep the person on just protein and calories. That is good for like 95% of people. However, if the person's liking engagement or you want more engagement or you want more accuracy out of a person, you can now hop into a full macronutrient distribution, which is protein, carbohydrate and fat. All right, specific numbers for each to increase the amount of accuracy you require of them and keep the progress taking along. And the easiest way for you to set a distribution for them, all right, is that you know that they've been eating the same amount of calories the whole time. But since they've been weighing themselves every day, you have that weight data. And since they've been logging in my fitness pal since, what is it, week four, right, because they've been tracking their body weight and their calorie and taken a calorie tracker this whole time, you can see what their macronutrient distribution is based off of their my fitness pal data. So when you have them pull up that data, you can look and see what their log is like, see what their macronutrient distribution has been like. And then you can essentially take the average of that value and tweak things a little bit as you need to to have it fit the amount of calories the original template has been. Make sure to move the protein up to about 0.8. Make sure the fat is whatever the template they're on is supposed to be. And the rest you just fill in with carbohydrates essentially until they hit their calorie mark. Okay. And that's how you set their macronutrient values. It's essentially the macronutrient value, whatever template they have, except now they can fill in those macronutrient values with whatever foods they want. Prefer they eat it off the list, but not, you know, they don't have to. That's all you got to do. Just make sure they're eating the same amount of calories. And you can continue on with the intermittent fasting protocol. If you so choose, I think it's handy. I'm a fan. So now two weeks later, you see how they did with this new amount of freedom and added amount of responsibility. And if they're like, mmm, their fat's always a little bit high and their protein's always a little bit low and their carbs always a little bit, you know, at this point, what you could do is you can give the client a win instead of scolding them or trying to figure out a way for them to hit their macronutrients. Exactly. What you can do is just rearrange their protein carbohydrate and fat to the values that they're most likely going to hit. So long as the calories are still in check and the protein value is somewhere around point eight, it can be as low as point six, depending on the preferences per pound. Then you just rearrange their carbohydrate and fat to fit the distribution that they're most comfortable with eating. And then you know that from here on out, they should be able to hit those values with no problem. So to reiterate again, if like their fat is always high on their carbohydrates are always low or vice versa, then you can just rearrange their protein carbohydrate and fat allotment to match those values that they were off and bingo, bingo, they're not winning. So hope that makes sense. Intermittent fasting, protein carbohydrate and fat distribution, and then rearranging their protein carbohydrate and fat to fit their preferences. And then we're on to the final phase.
